subject: Mathematics
options: <math><msqrt><mn>5</mn></msqrt></math> is not an integer <math><mn>5</mn></math> is not irrational
<math><msqrt><mn>5</mn></msqrt></math> is not an integer and <math><mn>5</mn></math> is not irrational
<math><msqrt><mn>5</mn></msqrt></math> is irrational or <math><mn>5</mn></math> is an integer
solution: Using<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>~</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi></math>, we get negation of given statement as <math><msqrt><mn>5</mn></msqrt></math> is not an integer and <math><mn>5</mn></math> is not irrational Number.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Using logical equivalence condition p→q≡~p∨q, given statement can be expressed as, 

p∧~q→p→~q

≡ ~p∧~q∨~p∨~q.......(1)

We know De Morgan's law of logical equivalence is,

~p∧q≡~p∨~q

Applying De Morgan's law in equation(1) we get,

≡ ~p∨q∨~p∨~q   ∵~~p≡p

≡ ~p∨~p∨q∨~q

≡~p∨t≡t (tautology)

subject: Mathematics

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Logical Statement p→q is false if and only if p is true and q is false.

So, when p is false, q is true, p∨q→p∨~q will be false.

Clearly p∨q→p∨~q is not a tautology.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
The given statement is p∧~q∨~p

Let us take A∩Bc∪Ac.

Now let us apply distributive law to the above statement.

≡A∪Ac∩Bc∪Ac

≡U∩Bc∪Ac

≡Bc∪Ac

Now the given statement p∧~q∨~p will be equivalent to ~q∨~p or ~p∨~q.

Hence the given statement is equivalent to ~p∨~q,

Now negation of given statement by demorgan's law will be,

~~p∨~q≡p∧q

subject: Mathematics
solution:
From De Morgan's Law,

~p∨q=~p∧~q

p→q=~p∧~q

~p∧q→p∨q

=~~p∧q∧~p∧~q

=~~p∧f

=~f=t, where f is contradiction and t is tautology.

subject: Mathematics
options: The contrapositive of $3 x+2=8 \Rightarrow x=2$ is $x \neq 2$ $\Rightarrow 3 x+2 \neq 8$.
The converse of $\tan \mathrm{x}=0 \Rightarrow x=0$ is $x \neq 0 \Rightarrow$ $\tan x=0$.
$p \vee q$ and $p \wedge q$ have the same truth table.
solution:
Only statement given in option
(a) is true.
(b) The converse of $\tan \mathrm{x}=0 \Rightarrow x=0$ is $x=0 \Rightarrow \tan x=0$
$\therefore$ Statement (b) is false
(c) $\sim(p \Rightarrow q$ is dquivalent to $p \wedge \sim q$
$\therefore$ Statement given in option (c) is false.
(d) No, $p \vee q$ and $p \wedge q$ does not have the same truth value.

subject: Mathematics
solution: As the truth table for the $(p \wedge \sim q) \wedge(p \wedge r)$ $\rightarrow \sim p \vee q$ is false, then only possible values of $(p, q, r)$ is $(T, F, T)$ $\begin{array}{llllll}p & q & r & \sim q & p \wedge \sim q & p \wedge r \\ \sim p & \sim p \vee q & (p \wedge \sim q) \wedge(p \wedge r)(p \wedge \sim q) \wedge(p \wedge r) \\ \rightarrow \sim p \vee q\end{array}$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
LHS of all the options are same i.e. ((P→Q)∧~Q)

≡(~P∨Q)∧~Q

≡(~P∧~Q)∨(Q∧~Q)

≡~P∧~Q

(A) (~P∧~Q)→Q

≡~(~P∧~Q)∨Q

≡(P∨Q)∨Q≠ tautology

(B) (~P∧~Q)→~P

≡~(~P∧~Q)∨~P

≡(P∨Q)∨~P

(C) (~P∧~Q)→P

≡(P∨Q)∨P≠ Tautology

(D) (~P∧~Q)→(P∧Q)

≡(P∨Q)∨(P∧Q)≠ Tautology

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given,

(p∧(~q))∨((~p) ∧ q) ∨((~p) ∧ (~q))

=((~p) ∧ q) ∨((~p) ∧ (~q))∨(p∧(~q))

=~p∧q∨~q∨p∧~q

We know that ~A∨A=T, where T is tautology,

=~p∧T∨p∧~q

=~p∨p∧~q

=~p∨p∧~p∨~q

=T∧~p∨~q

=~p∨~q

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given condition is, p∧q→r∨q∧p∧r→q).....(1)

We know conditional disjunction as,

p→q≡~p∨q

Hence, p∧r→q≡~p∧r∨q

Using De Morgan's law

p∧r→q≡~p∧r∨q≡~p∨~r∨q

Equation 1 can be written as,

⇒p∧q→r∨q∧~p∨~r∨q)

⇒~p∧q∨r∨q∧~p∨~r∨q)

⇒~p∨~q∨r∨q∧~p∨~r∨q)

⇒(~p∨~q∨r∨q∧~p∨~r∨q)

⇒~p∨t∧~p∨~r∨q)

⇒~p∨~r∨q

For above statement to be a tautology,

r can be ~p or q

When r=~p or r=q⇒~p∨~r∨q=t

∴ Two value of r are possible

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given,
p: Ramesh listens to music.
q: Ramesh is out of his village
r: It is Sunday
s: It is Saturday

Then the statement "Ramesh listens to music only if he is in his village and it is Sunday or Saturday" can be expressed as

Now on breaking each statement and combining them we get,

p≡ Ramesh listens to music

~q≡ He is in village.

r∨s≡ Saturday or Sunday

So, on combining all statement we get,

p⇒~q∧r∨s

=(~p∨q)∧(~q∨~p)  {p⇒q=~p∨q}

=(~p∨q)∧(~p∨~q)  {commutative property}

=~p∨(q∧~q)   {distributive property}

=~p

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Recall p⇒q≡F  if and only if T⇒F≡F

So, p∧~q∧p∧r→~p∨q

Taking Option A : T∧F∧T∧T→F∨T

≡F∧T→T

≡F→T≡T

Hence, option A is false.

Taking Option B : F∧F∧F∧F→T∨T

≡F→T≡T

Hence, option B is false.

Taking Option C : T∧T∧T∧T→F∨F

≡T→F≡F

Hence, option C is correct.

Taking Option D : F∧T∧F∧F→T∨F

≡F∧F→T

≡F→T≡T

Hence, option D is false.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
We know that,

Converse of A⇒B is B⇒A

So, using the above formula we get,

Converse of ~p∧q⇒r 

≡r⇒(~p∧q)

≡~r∨~p∧q

≡~p∧q∨~r

≡~p∨~q∨~r as ~A∨B≡~A∧~B

≡p∨~q⇒~r

subject: Mathematics
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& p \rightarrow(q \rightarrow p)=\sim p \vee(q \rightarrow p) \\
& =\sim p \vee(\sim q \vee p) \quad \text { since } p \vee \sim p \text { is always true } \\
& =\sim p \vee p \vee q=p \rightarrow(p \vee q) .
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
$q$ is false and $[(p \wedge q) \leftrightarrow r]$ is true As $(p \wedge q)$ is false

$[$ False $\leftrightarrow r]$ is true

Hence $r$ is false

Option (1): says $p \vee r$,

Since $r$ is false

Hence $(p \vee r)$ can either be true or false

Option (2): says $(p \wedge r) \rightarrow(p \vee r)$

$(p \wedge r)$ is false

Since, $F \rightarrow T$ is true and

$F \rightarrow F$ is also true

Hence, it is a tautology

Option (3): $(p \vee r) \rightarrow(p \wedge r)$

i.e. $(p \vee r) \rightarrow F$

It can either be true or false

Option (4): $(p \wedge r)$

Since, $r$ is false

Hence, $(p \wedge r)$ is false.
